paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IV. Consecutive holes
summary: No resolution of the conjecture was found in the post-2018 literature. The source paper itself establishes the $k=3$ base case (every infinite set with bounded gaps is 3-constricting), and the related arXiv:1705.04609 (Scott–Seymour, Part X of the same series) handles arithmetic progressions as a special case of bounded-gap sets, but the full conjecture—that every infinite set with bounded gaps is $k$-constricting for every $k$—appears to remain open.

internal_refs_verified.note: Title is 'Some results and problems on tournament structure' by Nguyen, Scott, Seymour. The paper concerns chromatic number of tournaments, vertex-disjoint subtournaments, and domination number. It has no connection to Conjecture 1.5, which is about constricting sets, bounded-gap integer sets, and holes in undirected graphs. This appears to be a false match (fuzz=67).

search_queries: Scott Seymour "constricting" "bounded gaps" chromatic number holes induced subgraphs
arXiv 1509.06563 Scott Seymour "consecutive holes" conjecture 1.5 follow-up
Scott Seymour "constricting" induced subgraphs chromatic number holes graph 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024
notes: No follow-up found after exhausting 5 web calls. The conjecture is relatively recent (journal 2018) and no paper explicitly proving or disproving it was located. The closely related arXiv:1705.04609 (Part X, published Combinatorica 2019) proves that sets of holes of a fixed residue modulo k form constricting sets, which is a special case of bounded-gap sets, but it predates the 2018 journal publication threshold for since_posted. The internal reference arXiv:2306.02364 was verified and is unrelated.
